summary: Dot voting is a simple, efficient technique for gathering group input on rankings or selections during meetings and workshops. The method involves posting options on a wall, giving each participant a fixed number of votes to place as dots next to their preferred items, and tallying results. Participants can vote in any order and concentrate multiple votes on single items to show strong preferences. Fowler has used this technique for various purposes including prioritizing discussion topics and even choosing terminology for the Agile Manifesto.
key_points: Dot voting enables quick, parallel voting without requiring people to take turns
Allowing multiple votes on one item lets participants express intensity of preference
The technique is versatile - useful for prioritization, selection, and ranking decisions
Physical visibility (wall-mounted lists) creates transparency in the voting process
money_quotes: From time to time during a meeting or workshop, it's good to get votes on a number things in order to rank or choose a subset.
Don't make people take turns or anything - just say something like 'you get five votes each'.
I also let people put more than one vote on one item if they want to show a strong preference.
key_insight: Effective group decision-making can be achieved through simple, low-ceremony techniques that enable parallel participation and allow expression of preference intensity.
